text: The adrenal gland is a small gland that sits just above the kidney in the back of the abdomen. Just like you have two kidneys, you have two adrenal glands. The purpose of the adrenal gland is to secrete hormones that affect the way your body manages blood pressure, salt and kidney function, as well as stress. Occasionally, people can develop tumors of the adrenal gland. Although these tumors are usually benign, they can sometimes cause over-secretion of one or more of the hormones produced by the adrenal gland. When this happens, the treatment for this is to remove the adrenal gland that has the tumor. This is called an adrenalectomy.

This video is of a real adrenalectomy performed at TriHealth. It is being performed minimally invasively using robotic technology. This video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical adrenalectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how the surgery is actually performed. If you are scheduled to have this procedure, your surgery will be performed in a similar fashion.

Robotic instruments are visible as the surgeon begins the procedure by identifying the splenic flexure of the colon, which is adjacent to the spleen. The transverse colon is covered by the omentum, a layer of fat. The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery instrument to mobilize the spleen and distal pancreas, moving them medially to expose the retroperitoneal adrenal gland. The hook electrocautery is used to cauterize and divide tissue, facilitating the exposure of the adrenal gland.

The surgeon separates the layers of fat between the inferior border of the pancreas and the colon, visible on the right side of the screen, to expose the adrenal gland. The adrenal gland is partially visible, appearing as a yellowish-orange organ distinct from the surrounding abdominal fat.

The surgeon exposes the adrenal gland, which is located above the kidney, by clearing the area to visualize the renal vein and other blood vessels. The adrenal tumor is visible, appearing larger than a normal adrenal gland due to its benign nature. The pancreas and spleen are lifted off the adrenal tumor to access it, revealing the orange color of the adrenal gland compared to the surrounding yellow fat and pancreas tissue.

The surgeon has fully exposed the adrenal tumor, lifting the spleen and distal pancreas off of it. The renal vein, which drains the left kidney, and the adrenal vein, which drains the adrenal gland, are visible. The adrenal vein is a small vein running vertically, while the renal vein is larger and runs horizontally. The surgeon prepares to divide the adrenal vein while maintaining the integrity of the renal vein, ensuring clear visualization of the renal vein's superior border.

The surgeon clears tissue around the adrenal vein to place clips for ligation and division. After dividing the vein, the surgeon separates surrounding tissue from the adrenal gland, holding its inferior pole with the left hand. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon cauterizes and divides tissue, ensuring removal of surrounding fat with the adrenal gland. The kidney becomes visible at the bottom right of the screen, appearing as a reddish-brown organ.
The surgeon removes the surrounding fat from the diaphragm and retroperitoneal muscles, ensuring complete clearance of tissue as the adrenal tumor is excised.

The surgeon divides the remaining tissue to free the adrenal gland. A bag or pouch is introduced through a trocar to encase the adrenal gland for removal. The spleen and pancreas are repositioned, completing the robotic left adrenalectomy.
